howItWorks.intro: Fuel cost comes down to three numbers: how far you drive, how many miles your car squeezes from a gallon, and what you pay at the pump. Multiply the gallons you burn by the pump price and you have your answer.
howItWorks.outro: Enter one-way miles to see the one-way cost; the round-trip figure doubles it. Your real MPG on the day depends on speed, wind, cargo, and how often you run the AC, so treat the total as a close planning estimate rather than an exact receipt.

faq.question: How do I split the fuel cost fairly among passengers?
faq.answer: Add up the total round-trip fuel cost, then divide by the number of people sharing the ride, usually including the driver. A 700-mile round trip at 30 MPG with $3.40/gal gas burns about 23.3 gallons and costs roughly $79. Split four ways that's about $20 each. If the driver covers wear and tear separately, some groups split fuel among passengers only and leave the driver out.

faq.question: Does running the air conditioning really lower my MPG?
faq.answer: Yes, but less than most people think on the highway. AC pulls engine power and can cut MPG by roughly 3 to 8 percent, more in stop-and-go city driving and hot weather. On a 400-mile highway leg at 32 MPG, a 5 percent hit costs you about half a gallon, or under $2 at $3.50/gal. Below about 45 mph open windows are often more efficient; above that the extra drag usually costs more than the AC.

faq.question: Why is my highway MPG different from my city MPG?
faq.answer: City driving means constant braking and accelerating, plus idling at lights, which wastes fuel. Highway driving holds a steady speed, so most cars get 20 to 40 percent better MPG on the highway. A car rated 24 city and 33 highway will cost noticeably more per mile in town. If your trip is mostly interstate, enter the highway number; if it's mixed, use a value in between or your car's combined rating.

faq.question: How much does a roof rack or cargo box cut my range?
faq.answer: A lot at highway speed. An empty roof rack adds drag worth roughly 1 to 5 percent, and a loaded rooftop cargo box can drop highway MPG by 10 to 25 percent because it wrecks the car's aerodynamics. On a 600-mile trip at 30 MPG that could mean burning 2 to 4 extra gallons. Lower your MPG input by 10 to 20 percent when you're running a full roof box to get a realistic total.

faq.question: How do headwinds and high speed change the numbers?
faq.answer: Air resistance climbs sharply with speed, so driving 75 mph instead of 65 can cut MPG by 10 to 15 percent, and a stiff headwind adds to that. If your car normally gets 32 MPG but you're pushing 78 mph into a 20 mph headwind, plug in something closer to 26 to 27 MPG. The extra fuel on a long interstate day can add $10 to $20 to the trip.

faq.question: What would the same trip cost in an electric car?
faq.answer: EVs are priced in kWh, not gallons. Most EVs use about 0.28 to 0.35 kWh per mile. A 500-mile trip at 0.30 kWh/mi uses 150 kWh; charging at $0.14/kWh at home costs about $21, versus roughly $62 for a 28 MPG gas car at $3.50/gal. Road-trip fast charging runs higher, often $0.30 to $0.50/kWh, which would push the same 150 kWh to $45 to $75, closer to gas.

faq.question: Should I enter one-way or round-trip miles?
faq.answer: Enter your one-way distance. The calculator shows the one-way fuel cost and a separate round-trip figure that simply doubles it, so you can see both. If your route home is different from the route out, or you're doing a loop, add up the full mileage and enter that total as your distance instead.

howWeCalculate.intro: The math behind your fuel cost is short, and every number stays in your browser. Here is exactly what happens when you hit Calculate.
steps: Divide your trip distance by your vehicle's MPG to find the gallons you'll burn — 500 miles ÷ 28 MPG is about 17.9 gallons.
Multiply those gallons by the pump price to get the one-way fuel cost — 17.9 gallons × $3.50 is about $62.50.
Double the one-way cost for the round-trip figure, and divide fuel cost by distance to show cost per mile.
Adjust your MPG input down for headwinds, roof boxes, AC, or high speed, then recalculate to see the real-world total.
howWeCalculate.disclaimer: This is a planning estimate. Actual gallons burned vary with terrain, traffic, weather, tire pressure, and driving style, and pump prices change by state and day.

whenToUse.intro: Reach for this calculator any time a trip means driving and you want the fuel number before you leave.
scenarios: Budgeting a weekend or cross-country drive before you book hotels and food
Splitting gas with friends so everyone chips in a fair share for the round trip
Comparing two routes — a shorter toll road versus a longer scenic one — by cost per mile
Deciding whether to take the fuel-efficient sedan or the thirsty SUV for a group trip
Checking whether an EV rental or your gas car is cheaper for a specific long-haul route
Estimating how much a loaded roof box or towing will add to a summer vacation drive

sections.title: The Three Numbers That Set Your Fuel Cost
sections.body: Every road trip fuel estimate rests on distance, MPG, and pump price. Take the miles you'll drive, divide by your car's miles per gallon to get gallons, and multiply by the price per gallon. A 600-mile trip at 30 MPG burns 20 gallons; at $3.40/gal that's $68 one way and $136 round trip. Change any one number and the total moves — a car that gets 24 MPG instead of 30 on that same trip burns 25 gallons and costs $85 each way. The calculator does this instantly and also shows your cost per mile, which is handy for comparing vehicles or routes.

sections.title: Why Your Real MPG Is Lower Than the Sticker
sections.body: The MPG on the window sticker is a lab number. On an actual trip, several things pull it down. Highway speeds above 65 mph, headwinds, a roof rack or cargo box, a full load of passengers and luggage, running the AC in summer heat, and stop-and-go city stretches all cost fuel. A full rooftop box alone can drop highway MPG by 10 to 25 percent. If you want an honest total, enter an MPG a bit lower than the sticker — knock off 10 to 20 percent when you're loaded up and driving fast into the wind — and recalculate.

sections.title: Splitting the Cost and Comparing Gas to Electric
sections.body: Once you have the round-trip fuel total, splitting it is simple division: a $120 round trip shared among four people is $30 each. If you're weighing a gas car against an EV, the units change but the idea doesn't. EVs use kWh instead of gallons, roughly 0.30 kWh per mile, so a 500-mile trip uses about 150 kWh — around $21 charging at home at $0.14/kWh, or more like $45 to $75 on road-trip fast chargers. Run the gas numbers here, then compare against your electric rate to see which vehicle actually wins for your route.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Trip Distance
howToSteps.description: Type your one-way distance in miles. Pull it from your maps app for the exact route you plan to drive; the round-trip figure is calculated for you.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Vehicle MPG
howToSteps.description: Use your car's combined rating, or the highway number for mostly-interstate drives. Trim it 10 to 20 percent if you'll be loaded, fast, running a roof box, or fighting a headwind.

howToSteps.title: Enter the Gas Price
howToSteps.description: Put in the price per gallon you expect to pay. Prices vary by state, so check a fuel app for your route rather than assuming your home price.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit Calculate to see gallons needed, one-way fuel cost, cost per mile, and round-trip cost all at once.

howToSteps.title: Adjust and Split
howToSteps.description: Tweak MPG or price to test scenarios, then divide the round-trip total by the number of people to get each passenger's share.

tips: Enter one-way miles and read the round-trip line — don't double the distance yourself or you'll quadruple the cost.
Drop your MPG input by 10 to 20 percent when a rooftop cargo box is loaded; aerodynamic drag is the biggest highway fuel penalty.
For a fair passenger split, divide the round-trip fuel total by everyone sharing the ride, including or excluding the driver as your group agrees.
Use your highway MPG for interstate trips and combined MPG for mixed routes — city driving can be 20 to 40 percent thirstier.
Check a fuel-price app for gas along your actual route; a 50-cent difference per gallon adds up fast over 20 gallons.
Slowing from 75 to 65 mph often recovers 10 to 15 percent of your MPG, worth real money on a long interstate day.
